verses.commentary: Vineyard parable continues the 'first-last' theme. Landowner goes early to hire day laborers. Day-labor culture: anxious, contingent existence.
Agreed wage: one denarius per day — standard daily wage. Contract clear, signed early.
Third hour (9 a.m.) — finds others idle in the marketplace. Hires them with no specific wage promised — 'whatever is right.'
Sends them to the vineyard on faith in his fairness.
Sixth hour (noon) and ninth (3 p.m.) — same pattern. Repeated hiring without fixed wage.
Eleventh hour (5 p.m., one hour of light left) — finds yet others standing idle. Why standing idle? Asks.
'No one hired us.' The unemployed are unemployed because no one chose them. The landowner ends their idleness with one hour of work.
Evening payday. Foreman instructed: pay last hired first. The reversed order matters — the early workers see the late workers' wages.
Eleventh-hour workers receive a denarius — a full day's wage for one hour. Astonishing generosity.
First workers expect proportionally more. Receive the same denarius. The math seems unjust to them.
They grumble (ego-gguzon, ἐγόγγυζον — same word as Israel in the wilderness, Exodus 16:2). Discontent in covenant memory.
'These last worked one hour, and you made them equal to us who bore the burden and the scorching heat.' Their objection is equality-grievance. Their work was longer, harder, sun-baked.
Landowner addresses one: 'Friend (hetaire, ἑταῖρε — neutral or cool form of address), I do you no wrong. Didn't you agree to a denarius?' The contract is intact.
'Take what is yours and go. I choose to give to this last as I gave to you.' Sovereign generosity is the landowner's prerogative.

'Am I not allowed to do what I want with what is mine? Or is your eye evil (ophthalmos sou poneros, ὀφθαλμός σου πονηρός — Hebraic idiom for envy) because I am generous?' Generosity exposes the grumblers' envy. Grace given to others wounds the resentful.
'So the last will be first, and the first last.' Restates the theme from 19:30. Kingdom economics: not seniority-based, not merit-tracked, but governed by the master's free generosity.
Going up to Jerusalem (geographic and theological ascent). Takes the Twelve aside privately.

Third passion prediction (compare 16:21, 17:22-23) — most detailed yet. 'Behold, we are going up to Jerusalem. Son of Man will be delivered to chief priests and scribes; they will condemn him to death.'

'And deliver him to the Gentiles to be mocked and flogged and crucified, and on the third day be raised.' Specifics: Gentile execution (Roman, not Jewish), crucifixion (not stoning). The cross detail finally explicit.
Mother of Zebedee's sons (later named Salome — Matthew 27:56) approaches with sons. Kneels to make a request. Maternal lobbying.
'Grant that my two sons may sit, one at your right and one at your left, in your kingdom.' Right and left in royal court — the highest status positions. Privatized eschatology.

'You do not know what you are asking. Can you drink the cup I am about to drink?' Cup (poterion, ποτήριον) — Old Testament wrath imagery (Isaiah 51:17; Jeremiah 25:15). They presume to share his throne; do they grasp his cross?

'You will drink my cup.' James will be martyred (Acts 12:2), John will suffer. 'But to sit at my right or left is not mine to grant — it has been prepared by my Father.' The Son defers to the Father's prerogative.
Ten others hear, indignant (eganaktesan, ἠγανάκτησαν). The indignation is itself a marker that they share the same ambition.

Jesus calls them: 'Rulers of the Gentiles lord it over (katakyrieuousin, κατακυριεύουσιν) them; great ones exercise authority (katexousiazousin, κατεξουσιάζουσιν) over them.' Pagan power model described.
'Not so among you. Whoever would be great must be your servant (diakonos, διάκονος).' The kingdom inverts the power gradient. Greatness measured by service rendered.
'Whoever would be first must be your slave (doulos, δοῦλος).' Stronger word — total disposability for others' good. The leader is the lowest.

'The Son of Man came not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom (lytron, λύτρον) for many (anti pollon, ἀντὶ πολλῶν).' Isaiah 53 substitutionary atonement summarized in one sentence. Anti pollon = in place of many. The cross is service at its costliest, and the model for all Christian greatness.
Leaving Jericho — fifteen miles from Jerusalem. Crowd follows. The procession to Passover is forming.

Two blind men by the road. (Matthew's doubling again, where Mark 10:46 has only Bartimaeus.) They call: 'Lord, Son of David, have mercy on us!' Davidic Messiah confessed by blind men who see truer than seeing critics.
Crowd rebukes them — silence the embarrassing. They cry out louder. Faith does not respect crowd-suppression.
Jesus stops, calls them. 'What do you want me to do for you?' He elicits the explicit request. Dignity in being asked.
'Lord, let our eyes be opened.' Direct petition. The healing requested is precise.

Pity (splagchnistheis, σπλαγχνισθεὶς) — gut compassion. Touches their eyes, sight given, they follow him. Recipients become disciples. The narrative of the journey to Jerusalem ends with blind men leading the procession in.
